texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper studies the continuous-time q-learning (the continuous time counterpart of Qlearing) for Markov switching system under Tsallis entropy regularization. We address the difficulty in traditional RL algorithms where the Tsallis entropy regularization leads to an optimal policy distribution not necessarily a Gibbs measure, which often complicates algorithm design. Furthermore, to address the limited universality of current continuous time regime-switching RL algorithms (often restricted to the EMV framework), this study focuses on continuous-time q-learning for Markov regime-switching systems based on Tsallis entropy, aiming for a more universally applicable continuous-time RL method. We establish the martingale characterization of the q-function under Tsallis entropy for continuous-time Markov regime-switching systems. Based on this, we design two q-learning algorithms, distinguished by whether the Lagrange multiplier can be explicitly derived. We apply these algorithms to the continuous-time exploratory Mean-Variance (EMV) portfolio optimization problem in a regime-switching market. Numerical experiments demonstrate the satisfactory performance of our q-learning algorithms.

We establish the martingale characterization of the q-function under Tsallis entropy for continuous-time Markov regime-switching systems. Based on this, we design two q-learning algorithms, distinguished by whether the Lagrange multiplier can be explicitly derived. We apply these algorithms to the continuous-time exploratory Mean-Variance (EMV) portfolio optimization problem in a regime-switching market. Numerical experiments demonstrate the satisfactory performance of our q-learning algorithms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, the theory and application of Reinforcement Learning (RL) have seen significant advancements within continuous-time frameworks. These frameworks have demonstrated particular advantages when addressing complex dynamic systems. As a foundational algorithm in reinforcement learning, Q-learning(see Watkins 1989, Watkins and Dayan 1992) evaluates the long-term value of state-action pairs using a Q-function and improves policies based on this evaluation. Traditionally, Q-learning has been applied to discrete-time Markov decision processes (MDPs). In this context, the algorithm learns a Q-function that maps state-action pairs to their expected returns, allowing for policy updates by selecting actions that maximize future rewards (Sutton 2018). However, applying this traditional approach to continuous-time scenarios presents numerous challenges. The Q-function is essentially a discrete-time concept that collapses into a value function independent of actions in continuous-time settings; thus, it cannot be used for ranking and selecting current actions. Tallec et al. (2019) even argues that 'there is no Q-function in continuous time.'

texts.orig: The common approach is to discretize continuous time to obtain a discretized Q-function, then apply existing Q-learning algorithms to address continuous-time problems. Nevertheless, empirical evidence indicates that this method is highly sensitive to the choice of time discretization and performs poorly with small time steps. Instead of discretizing, Kim et al. (2021) took a different approach by integrating actions as state variables within continuous-time systems. This method ensures that the motion process remains continuously smooth over time with a bounded growth rate. However, they only consider deterministic dynamic systems and discretize the continuoustime problem, requiring actions to be absolutely continuous, which has significant limitations in real-world applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address the limitations of traditional Q-learning in continuous-time settings, the pioneering work of Wang et al. (2020) and Jia and Zhou (2022a,b, 2023) established a theoretical foundation for continuous-time reinforcement learning. Their research introduced a series of important theories for situations with continuous state spaces (diffusion processes) and potentially continuous action spaces. Wang et al. (2020) introduced an exploratory stochastic control framework that incorporates entropy regularization. They achieved this by formulating an entropy-regularized, distribution-valued stochastic control problem for diffusion processes, which enables the control to be chosen strategically rather than in a predetermined or non-adaptive manner. Jia and Zhou (2022a) focuses on the policy evaluation (PE) problem, which involves learning the value function of a given random policy and characterizing it as a martingale problem. Building upon this, Jia and Zhou (2022b) subsequently addressed the policy gradient (PG) problem, which involves computing the gradient of the learned value function with respect to the current policy. They demonstrated that the PG problem is mathematically reducible to a simpler policy evaluation (PE) problem, which can then be solved using the martingale approach developed in Jia and Zhou (2022a). These theoretical contributions have enabled the development of various online and offline Actor-Critic (AC) algorithms for model-free RL under diffusion dynamics. Significantly, many of these derived algorithms provide novel interpretations of, or even recover, well-known algorithms from the discrete-time Markov decision process setting. In a significant advancement, Jia and Zhou (2023) further proposed the continuous-time q-learning method. This approach extends the traditional Q-function and Q-learning algorithms to continuous time. The extension is achieved by utilizing a first-order approximation of the advantage function, which is defined as the temporal difference between the Q-function and the value function. Compared with discrete-time RL algorithms, continuous-time RL methods design policy iteration rules and loss functions for policy evaluation directly within a continuous-time framework, without requiring any prior full discretization. This allows the algorithm to maintain greater stability and robustness during execution, particularly when compared to methods that are sensitive to the chosen discretization step.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The continuous-time RL framework also facilitates the application of advanced mathematical tools and techniques, such as stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and control theory, which are essential for establishing a solid theoretical foundation for the algorithms. In recent years, continuous-time RL theories and algorithms have been extended to address a variety of new problems. For instance, Wang et al. (2023) proposed an entropy-regularized Actor-Critic RL algorithm to address the optimal execution problem within the continuous-time Almgren-Chriss model. Specifically, the study formulated the optimal execution strategy by constructing a relaxed stochastic control problem under an entropy-regularized mean-variance objective. Dai et al. (2023) applied RL to the Merton's utility maximization problem in an incomplete markets, focusing on learning optimal portfolio strategies without requiring knowledge of model parameters. Bo et al. (2023) studied the infinite-horizon optimal tracking portfolio problem with capital injection in an incomplete market. Notably, for the case of unknown parameters, they introduced an entropyregularized exploratory formulation and proposed a continuous-time q-learning algorithm based on a reflected diffusion process to determine the optimal tracking strategy. Han et al. (2023) integrated the Shannon regularization term into continuous-time entropy regularized RL, deriving explicit solutions for optimal strategies within the Linear-Quadratic (LQ) setting. Wei et al. (2024) extended the continuous-time q-learning algorithm to mean-field control problems where the integrated qfunction and the essential q-function together with test policies play crucial roles in their model free algorithm. Giegrich et al. (2024) investigate the global linear convergence of PG methods for exploratory LQ control problems in continuous time. Their work employs geometry-aware gradient descents and proposes a novel algorithm specifically for discrete-time policies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Within the study of continuous-time Q-learning, entropy regularization is an significant direction, with Shannon entropy being a commonly used entropy regularization method. Moreover, Tsallis (1988) introduced a generalization of the Shannon entropy known as the Tsallis entropy, which offers greater flexibility and robustness to handling learning tasks with diverse policy distributions. Tsallis entropy is particularly advantageous in scenarios involving non-Gaussian, heavy-tailed distributions within a compact support setting. By adjusting the index parameters of Tsallis entropy regularization, it can transform learned optimal policies into various types, providing greater flexibility in handling uncertainty and incentivizing exploration. Notably, Lee et al. (2018, 2019) have extensively studied a class of Markov decision processes (MDPs) with Tsallis entropy maximization. Specifically, Lee et al. (2018) introduced causal sparse Tsallis entropy regularization to propose sparse MDPs. Meanwhile, Lee et al. (2019) developed Tsallis MDPs, which utilize an additional entropy index parameter to provide a unified framework for entropy-regularized RL problems, including the well-known standard Shannon-Gibbs (SG) entropy. By controlling the entropy index, Tsallis MDPs can generate different categories of optimal policies. Besides, Donnelly and Jaimungal (2024) recently investigated the optimal control problems in models with latent factors, where the agent controls the distribution of behavior through Tsallis entropy rewards for exploration in both discrete and continuous time. Therefore, this paper considers the Tsallis entropy to encourage exploration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In many real-world scenarios, phenomena or systems exhibit properties of state transitions or trend changes. Mathematically, these situations are often modeled using Markov chains. In this context, the state transitions of the Markov chain represent changes in market trends. Consequently, Markov chains can effectively simulate random fluctuations in real-world environments, better capturing sudden shifts and uncertainties within a dynamic setting. Considering this, Zhou and Yin (2003) investigated the continuous-time mean-variance (MV) problem with regimeswitching, although their work did not integrate it with reinforcement learning. More recently, Wang et al. (2020) proposed the exploratory mean-variance (EMV) framework. This approach replaces deterministic strategies with probability distributions and incorporates entropy regularization into the portfolio optimization problem. They solved for the optimal investment strategy, finding it to be a probability distribution over the control space rather than a deterministic control function. Recent research by Wu and Li (2024) considered applying reinforcement learning to the continuous-time mean-variance portfolio problem in a regime-switching market. They formulated the problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) with two unobservable market regimes and addressed the partial observability using the Wonham filter. However, their approach relies on several key assumptions: they assume state-independent volatility, and their market parameters must either be known or estimated from data. Specifically, the martingale loss function employed by Wu and Li (2024) requires knowledge of the true market parameters, which is impractical in real-world applications. To address these limitations, Chen et al. (2025) investigated the exploratory mean-variance with regime switching (EMVRS) problem. Their model extends the existing framework by allowing for state-dependent volatility. Crucially, their parameter update scheme utilizes the orthogonality condition loss derived from the optimal EMVRS value function. The advantage of this loss function is that it does not depend on the true market parameters, allowing the RL algorithm to directly learn and identify the latent market parameters from the training data. Simulation studies further confirm that, through this method, the parameters of the proposed RL model converge to their corresponding true values, even when initialized from randomly selected starting points.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continuous-time Q-learning is still developing, especially concerning entropy regularization. Current RL algorithms predominantly employ Shannon entropy because the resulting regularized and normalized optimal policy is essentially a Gaussian distribution. The ability to explicitly determine its mean and variance greatly simplifies subsequent algorithm design. However, a major challenge with general entropy regularization methods is that the regularized and normalized optimal policy may not be a determined distribution form, which fundamentally constrains the design of policy-based algorithms. To sidestep this issue, some research shifts to value-based algorithms, such as the temporal difference (TD) learning and orthogonality condition (OC) learning (martingale loss learning) explored in Chen et al. (2025). Nonetheless, policy-based algorithms must directly solve the optimal policy characterization problem. Furthermore, studies on continuous-time Q-learning incorporating regime-switching mechanisms remain limited. Therefore, this paper introduces Tsallis entropy to encourage exploration under a regime-switching framework and develops a corresponding continuous-time q-learning method. Our framework extends the work presented in Wei et al. (2024) and Chen et al. (2025). Notably, our model reduces to that of Chen et al. (2025) when the Tsallis entropy order p is set to 1. Furthermore, it is worth emphasizing that our study aims to develop a more general continuous-time reinforcement learning method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we formulate an entropyregularized, exploratory RL framework in continuous time and space for Markov regime-switching systems under Tsallis entropy, and present some useful preliminary results. In Section 3, we derive the corresponding q-function and establish its fundamental martingale properties, showing that the optimal policy is characterized by the dependence of the q-function on a Lagrange multiplier. In Section 4, we design the corresponding q-learning algorithms for two distinct cases where the Lagrange multiplier is known or unknown. In Section 5, we provide a practical example of the EMVRS problem, including the parametric forms of the optimal value and q-function, and demonstrate satisfactory convergence results through numerical experiments. Finally, Section 6 presents our conclusion. The Appendix contains supplementary materials and detailed proofs for the statements presented in the main text.
texts.text: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we formulate an entropyregularized, exploratory RL framework in continuous time and space for Markov regime-switching systems under Tsallis entropy, and present some useful preliminary results. In Section 3, we derive the corresponding q-function and establish its fundamental martingale properties, showing that the optimal policy is characterized by the dependence of the q-function on a Lagrange multiplier. In Section 4, we design the corresponding q-learning algorithms for two distinct cases where the Lagrange multiplier is known or unknown. In Section 5, we provide a practical example of the EMVRS problem, including the parametric forms of the optimal value and q-function, and demonstrate satisfactory convergence results through numerical experiments. Finally, Section 6 presents our conclusion. The Appendix contains supplementary materials and detailed proofs for the statements presented in the main text.

Let (Ω , F , F , P ) be a filtered probability space with the filtration F := {F t } t ∈ [0 ,T ] generated by a standard one-dimensional Brownian motion W = { W } t ∈ [0 ,T ] and a continuous time irreducible Markov chain α = { α ( t ) } t ∈ [0 ,T ] with a finite state space M = { 1 , 2 , . . . , L } such that W ( t ) and α ( t ) are independent of each other. We assume the generator of the Markov chain α is Q = ( q ij ) L × L . Let Φ j ( t ) be the number of jumps into state j up to time t and set ˜ Φ j ( t ) ≜ Φ j ( t ) -∫ t 0 ∑ L i = j q ij 1 { α s -= i } ( s )d s . Then for each j ∈ M , the process ˜ Φ j ( t ) is an ( F , P ) -martingale. For any i, j ∈ M and t ≥ s ≥ 0 , we further let p ij ( t, s ) denote the transition probability P ( α ( t ) = j | α ( s ) = i ) .

The agent learns through continuous trial-and-error, which involves testing a (continuous-time) action sequence a = { a s , t ≤ s ≤ T } , observing the corresponding state process X a = { X a s , t ≤ s ≤ T } , and iteratively updating and refining its actions based on these observations.
texts.text: Remark 2.2. In the RL setting, the agent has partial or complete ignorance of the environment (i.e., the functions b, σ, r, h ). The agent learns through continuous trial-and-error, which involves testing a (continuous-time) action sequence a = { a s , t ≤ s ≤ T } , observing the corresponding state process X a = { X a s , t ≤ s ≤ T } , and iteratively updating and refining its actions based on these observations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A key challenge lies in strategically generating the sequence of trial-and-error actions. The core idea is randomization: the agent designs and employs a stochastic policy, which is a probability distribution over the action space, to generate actions dependent on the current time-state-regime triplet. Notably, this randomization is independent of the Brownian motion W , which is the stochastic source of the original control problem and represents environmental noise. Specifically, we assume the probability space is sufficiently rich to support a uniform random variable process { Z t , 0 ≤ t ≤ T } independent of W (construction of this process may require extending the probability space; see Sun (2006)). We extend the filtered probability space to (Ω , F , P ; {F s } s ≥ 0 ) , where F s = F s W ∨ σ ( Z t , 0 ≤ t ≤ s ) . The probability measure P , now defined on F T , is an extension of P W (i.e., the measures coincide when restricted to F W T ).

We now consider utilizing the optimal Gaussian approximation (OGA) to solve the policy update problem min ϕ ′ ∈ Φ D KL ( π ϕ ′ ( a | t, x, i ) ‖ π ϕ ( a | t, x, i )) . The core method involves using a second order Taylor expansion to transform the complex KL divergence minimization into a local quadratic programming problem with a closed form solution. As the minimization of D KL ( π ϕ ′ ( a | t, x, i ) ‖ π ϕ ( a | t, x, i )) is generally intractable analytically, OGA yields an approximate closed form solution via a second order Taylor expansion of the target log density log π ϕ ( a | t, x, i ) near the current mean µ . We conduct simulations with the following configurations: We consider a one-year investment horizon ( T = 1) with 25 equal-step-size time points throughout the year for portfolio rebalancing (∆ t = 1 / 25) . The investor starts with x 0 = 1 and sets a target of z = 1 . 4 to be achieved by the end of the year. During the investment period, the investor explores the investment strategies with the exploration weight equal to γ = 0 . 5 . To simulate realistic market dynamics, we model the environment as a two-state continuous time Markov chain representing 'bull' ( i = 1) and 'bear' ( i = 2) market conditions and the initial state is randomly selected. The transitions between these regimes are governed by the generator matrix: Q = ( -1 1 1 -1 ) , where the transition intensities are λ 1 = 1 and λ 2 = 1 . Specifically, 'bull' is characterized by the drift µ 1 = 0 . 2 , volatility σ 1 = 0 . 2 , and the risk-free rate r 1 = 0 . 01 . Conversely, 'bear' reflects a more volatile environment with µ 2 = -0 . 2 , σ 2 = 0 . 3 , and r 2 = 0 . 05 . Accordingly, the ground truth parameters are defined as θ true = ( ρ 1 , true = 0 . 95 , ρ 2 , true = -0 . 833 , σ 1 , true = 0 . 2 , σ 2 , true = 0 . 3) . texts.orig: This study applies an identical learning rate schedule to both the value function parameters θ , the q -function parameters ζ to ensure synchronous convergence within the Actor-Critic framework. Since both parameters share the same financial space, this synchronized approach aligns the scales of Q -function estimation and asset allocation updates. In non-stationary environments with frequent regime switching, this mechanism minimizes training oscillations caused by estimation lag, thereby improving numerical robustness and convergence efficiency under complex market dynamics.
texts.text: This study applies an identical learning rate schedule to both the value function parameters θ , the q -function parameters ζ to ensure synchronous convergence within the Actor-Critic framework. Since both parameters share the same financial space, this synchronized approach aligns the scales of Q -function estimation and asset allocation updates. In non-stationary environments with frequent regime switching, this mechanism minimizes training oscillations caused by estimation lag, thereby improving numerical robustness and convergence efficiency under complex market dynamics.

We conduct simulations with the following configurations: We consider a one-year investment horizon ( T = 1) with 25 equal-stepsize time points throughout the year for portfolio rebalancing (∆ t = 1 / 25) . The investor starts with x 0 = 1 and sets a target of z = 1 . 4 to be achieved by the end of the year. To simulate realistic market dynamics, we model the environment as a two-state continuous time Markov chain representing 'bull' ( i = 1) and 'bear' ( i = 2) market conditions and the initial state is randomly selected. The transitions between these regimes are governed by the generator matrix: Q = ( -1 . 8 1 . 8 2 . 0 -2 . 0 ) , where the transition intensities are λ 1 = 1 . 8 and λ 2 = 2 . 0 . Specifically, 'bull' is characterized by the drift µ 1 = 0 . 12 , volatility σ 1 = 0 . 15 , and the risk-free rate r 1 = 0 . 02 . Conversely, 'bear' reflects a more volatile environment with µ 2 = -0 . 10 , σ 2 = 0 . 35 , and r 2 = 0 . 025 . Accordingly, the ground truth parameters are defined as θ true = ( ρ 1,true = 0 . 733 , ρ 2,true = -0 . 428 , σ 1,true = 0 . 15 , σ 2,true = 0 . 35) . texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Conclusion
texts.text: 6 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inspired by prior work on regime switching and recent advancements in stochastic control Reinforcement Learning (RL), this paper extends continuous-time q-learning theory by introducing the more general Tsallis entropy as the entropy regularizer.
texts.text: Inspired by prior work on regime switching and recent advancements in stochastic control Reinforcement Learning (RL), this paper extends continuous-time q-learning theory by introducing the more general Tsallis entropy as the entropy regularizer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Traditional RL algorithms typically favor Shannon entropy over more general entropic regularizers, such as Tsallis entropy. The primary difficulty is that the latter often yields an optimal policy distribution that is not in a standard probabilistic form, thereby limiting subsequent algorithm design; we overcome this challenge. Additionally, most current continuous-time regime-switching RL algorithms are considered within the EMV problem framework, rather than the more general stochastic control framework. This constraint restricts their applicability to other problem frameworks (e.g., LQ problems), thereby limiting the breadth of algorithm application. Given the significance of entropic regularization in continuous-time q-learning, and the need to effectively model sudden shifts and uncertainty in real environments, this work focuses on continuous-time q-learning for Markov regime-switching systems based on Tsallis entropy, aiming to provide a more universally applicable continuous-time RL algorithm.
texts.text: Traditional RL algorithms typically favor Shannon entropy over more general entropic regularizers, such as Tsallis entropy. The primary difficulty is that the latter often yields an optimal policy distribution that is not in a standard probabilistic form, thereby limiting subsequent algorithm design; we overcome this challenge. Additionally, most current continuous-time regime-switching RL algorithms are considered within the EMV problem framework, rather than the more general stochastic control framework. This constraint restricts their applicability to other problem frameworks (e.g., LQ problems), thereby limiting the breadth of algorithm application. Given the significance of entropic regularization in continuous-time q-learning, and the need to effectively model sudden shifts and uncertainty in real environments, this work focuses on continuous-time q-learning for Markov regime-switching systems based on Tsallis entropy, aiming to provide a more universally applicable continuous-time RL algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Specifically, we establish the martingale characterization of the q-function under Tsallis entropy for continuous-time systems with regime-switching. Based on this, we design two q-learning algorithms, classifying them by whether the Lagrange multiplier can be explicitly derived. When the Lagrange multiplier is intractable, we provide two distinct policy update methods: the update rule based on (4.5) (Algorithm 2) and the update rule based on KL divergence (4.6)(Algorithm 3).
texts.text: Specifically, we establish the martingale characterization of the q-function under Tsallis entropy for continuous-time systems with regime-switching. Based on this, we design two q-learning algorithms, classifying them by whether the Lagrange multiplier can be explicitly derived. When the Lagrange multiplier is intractable, we provide two distinct policy update methods: the update rule based on (4.5) (Algorithm 2) and the update rule based on KL divergence (4.6)(Algorithm 3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We apply these algorithms to the continuous-time exploratory Mean-Variance portfolio optimization problem in a regime-switching market, and observe the performance of Algorithms 2 when the normalizing function is unavailable. Notably, we find that for the Tsallis entropy parameter p = 1 , the optimal policy is explicitly characterized (Algorithm 1); however, for p > 1 , explicit characterization is difficult, prompting the use of Algorithm 2. Furthermore, as the coupled system of equations satisfied by the optimal value function coefficients is analytically intractable, we characterize and solve it numerically using a system of matrix equations.
